perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that due to the sensitive nature of the Confidential Informati on, any breach of this Agreement would cause irreparable ha rm to the Disclosing Party and that the Disclosing Party shall therefore be entitled to equitable relief in additi on to all other remedies available at law.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that due to the sensitive nature of the Confidential Information, any breach of this Agreement would cause harm to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change omits the Receiving Party's acknowledgement that a breach would cause 'irreparable harm' and removes the Disclosing Party's right to 'equitable relief' for a breach. Depending on the jurisdiction, this could be a legally required remedy for breach of contract, especially NDAs.
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by state, e.g., California Civil Code § 3366
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3366 states that specific or preventive relief may be given as provided by the laws of this state. By removing the explicit right to equitable relief, the contract suggests it is not available, contradicting the state law which permits it in certain cases of breach.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3366. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. General Principles [3366 - 3369] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) 3366. Specific or preventive relief may be given as provided by the laws of this state. (Amended by Stats. 1905, Ch. 465.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract acknowledges irreparable harm and the right to equitable relief. The changed contract omits this acknowledgement and right. The Law Explanation states that California Civil Code § 3366 allows for specific or preventive relief. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states "Specific or preventive relief may be given as provided by the laws of this state." By removing the explicit right to equitable relief, the contract contradicts state law because it suggests this remedy is not available, even though the law permits it.

perturbation.original_text: All negotiating, drafting and presentations of offe rs shall be handled through ROI Corporation, as AGE NT FOR THE VENDOR, on ROI Corporation’s standard offer doc umentation and that I will seek independent legal a dvice for review prior to issuing my signature.
perturbation.changed_text: All negotiating, drafting and presentations of offers shall be handled through ROI Corporation, as AGENT FOR THE VENDOR, on ROI Corporation’s standard offer documentation.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'I will seek independent legal advice for review prior to issuing my signature', the contract omits an important acknowledgement that the Receiving Party is encouraged to seek their own legal counsel. In some jurisdictions, a party must demonstrate they were advised to seek independent counsel, or had the opportunity to do so, for the contract to be fully enforceable, especially when dealing with complex transactions or potential conflicts of interest.
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by state, e.g., common law principles of contract fairness
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly codified, the principle of ensuring a party had the opportunity to seek independent legal advice is a common law concept related to contract fairness and enforceability. Removing the suggestion of independent review weakens the enforceability of the contract in situations where fairness is challenged.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The omission of the phrase 'I will seek independent legal advice for review prior to issuing my signature' creates a legal contradiction under the category of 'Omission Legal Contradiction'. This is because the omission removes an important acknowledgment that the Receiving Party is encouraged to seek their own legal counsel, weakening the enforceability of the contract if fairness is challenged. Snippet 1 mentions that a court may refuse to enforce a contract if it finds the contract to be unconscionable at the time it was made. By removing the suggestion of independent review, the modified contract may be seen as unconscionable, especially if the party did not have legal representation.
